title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Roll Again: A Brief History of Board Games
content.text: Long before screens dominated our leisure time, people gathered around flat surfaces to push pieces, draw cards and outwit one another. The history of board games stretches back at least five thousand years, making them among the oldest known forms of organised entertainment. (37) [37] Ancient boards have been found in tombs, temples and the ruins of ordinary homes, suggesting that games were a central part of daily life across many cultures. [/37] || The Royal Game of Ur, discovered in a Mesopotamian burial site and dated to around 2600 BC, is one of the oldest playable games ever found. Its rules were eventually deciphered from a Babylonian clay tablet. (38) [38] Senet, played in ancient Egypt, and the Chinese game of Go, thought to be over four thousand years old, show that complex strategic thinking was cultivated through play across entirely different civilisations. [/38] || Chess emerged in India around the sixth century AD and spread westward through Persia and the Arab world before reaching Europe. (39) [39] By the time it arrived in medieval Europe, it had already undergone significant changes to its rules and piece designs, shaped by each culture through which it had passed. [/39] The game we recognise today was standardised only in the fifteenth century. || The twentieth century brought an explosion of new game design. Monopoly, developed in the 1930s, became a global phenomenon. Scrabble, Cluedo and Risk each built large followings in subsequent decades. (40) [40] These games introduced new mechanics — economic simulation, word-building, deduction — that expanded what board games could do. [/40] || In recent decades, a new wave of 'designer games', originating largely in Germany, has reinvented the hobby. Games like Settlers of Catan and Ticket to Ride have attracted players who never previously considered themselves board game enthusiasts. (41) [41] Their emphasis on cooperation, strategy and accessible rules has broadened the audience dramatically. [/41] || Today, board games are enjoying a global renaissance, with dedicated cafés, annual conventions and a booming publishing industry. (42) [42] In an age of digital saturation, there is something deeply appealing about the simplicity of a board, some pieces and the company of other people. [/42]

paragraphs.A: This process of cultural transmission is typical of the most enduring games in history.
paragraphs.B: The game's longevity is a testament to the enduring human appetite for strategic challenge.
paragraphs.C: These ancient examples demonstrate that the urge to play, strategise and compete is deeply embedded in human nature.
paragraphs.D: Sales figures suggest that people are now spending more time on board games than at any point since the television era began.
paragraphs.E: Unlike video games, which often discourage conversation, board games require direct human interaction.
paragraphs.F: Each of these classics left a lasting mark on the design vocabulary of the hobby.
paragraphs.G: This new generation of games is often described as more collaborative and less punishing than their predecessors.

questions.explanation: C concludes that the ancient examples demonstrate that the impulse to play and compete is deeply rooted in human nature, supporting the initial statement about the oldest games.
B mentions the longevity of the game as proof of the human appetite for strategic challenge, connecting with the examples of games from different civilisations.
A describes cultural transmission as typical of the most enduring games, supporting how chess changed as it passed through different cultures.
F asserts that each of these classics left a lasting mark on the hobby design vocabulary, closing the paragraph about Monopoly, Scrabble and Risk.
G describes the new generation of games as more collaborative and less punitive, supporting the expansion of the audience mentioned.
E notes that board games require direct human interaction, unlike video games, supporting the appeal of games in the digital age.
